food_name: Puttu (steamed rice flour and grated coconut cake)

Puttu (steamed rice flour and grated coconut cake)

english: Puttu (steamed rice flour and grated coconut cake)
malay: Puttu (kek beras kukus dengan kelapa parut)

region_of_origin: Kerala, India (also popular in Tamil Nadu, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, and Singapore)
food_subgroup: Steamed rice flour preparations — cylindrical steamed cake of roasted rice flour layered with freshly grated coconut, cooked in a puttu kutti (cylindrical steamer)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for puttu (Kerala steamed rice flour and coconut cylinder). Closest parent would be a generic steamed rice cake concept. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dishes — closest FoodEx2 parent for steamed rice flour preparations; no specific puttu code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for puttu. A000E (rice-based dishes) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for puttu as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard puttu cylinder weighs approximately 150g and is the conventional single-serving portion as prepared in a puttu kutti steamer mould.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Puttu (steamed rice flour and coconut)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes. Values for puttu are drawn directly from this publication. Per-100g values are consistent with the ICMR-NIN composition data for steamed rice flour and coconut preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Foods — Steamed Rice Flour Preparations; analogous estimate based on roasted rice flour GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate for roasted rice flour steamed preparation)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for puttu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~65 (medium) is estimated based on the closest analogous food: steamed rice flour preparations. Roasted rice flour has a partially reduced GI compared to plain white rice (GI ~72) due to the roasting process, which partially gelatinises starch and reduces rapid digestibility. Coconut fat content further moderates glycaemic response. GL per 150g cylinder = (65 × 27g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.5) / 100 = 26.3. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (50mg per 100g; ~75mg per 150g cylinder)
hypertension.guidance: Puttu is a low-sodium food (50mg/100g) and does not pose a sodium concern for hypertensive individuals; however, accompaniments such as salted fish curry or pickles can significantly increase the sodium load of the meal and should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65) and high glycaemic load per cylinder (~26.3 GL) from rice flour carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Puttu alone carries a substantial glycaemic load (GL ~26.3 per 150g cylinder) due to its rice flour base; pairing with kadala curry (black chickpea curry) is strongly recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as the chickpea fibre (~5g) and protein (~8g) significantly lower the composite meal GI and blunt postprandial glucose response — whereas serving with palm sugar or banana markedly increases the glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Relatively high saturated fat content (6g/100g; ~9g per cylinder) from freshly grated coconut, predominantly medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs, lauric acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat in puttu (6g/100g) is predominantly coconut-derived MCTs (lauric acid), which have a different metabolic profile from long-chain saturated fats; cholesterol is zero and the food is low in sodium, but individuals with established cardiovascular disease should be mindful of total saturated fat intake across the meal and limit high-fat accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Puttu is a traditional Kerala breakfast food consisting of a cylindrical steamed cake made from roasted rice flour (puttu podi) layered with freshly grated coconut, cooked in a specialised cylindrical steamer called a puttu kutti. The result is a firm yet crumbly, mildly flavoured cylinder with alternating layers of white rice flour and white coconut. One standard cylinder weighs approximately 150g and provides 285 kcal, 6g protein, 12g fat (9g saturated from coconut), and 40.5g carbohydrate. The glycaemic index is approximately 65 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of ~26.3 per cylinder — clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The critical clinical point is that puttu's nutritional profile is dramatically improved when paired with kadala curry (black chickpea curry), which adds ~8g protein and ~5g fibre per serving and substantially lowers the composite meal GI. Puttu served with palm sugar or banana significantly increases the glycaemic burden and is unsuitable for T2DM. Puttu is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is widely available in Singapore's Little India at Kerala restaurants, Sri Lankan eateries, and South Indian tiffin shops, and is particularly common among Singapore's Malayalam-speaking community. Clinicians counselling Kerala Singaporeans on diabetes or cardiovascular risk should specifically enquire about the puttu accompaniment, as this single variable dramatically changes the meal's clinical profile.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods), which is the approved primary regional source for Indian dishes. Values are consistent with the known composition of roasted rice flour and freshly grated coconut. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, with confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for puttu (roasted rice flour and grated coconut) are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA FoodData Central does not contain a direct puttu entry to supplement this data. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: same reason — fatty acid sub-fractions not reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation; no approved source provides this breakdown for puttu specifically. water_g set to -1: moisture content for puttu as a steamed preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, and no approved source provides a direct moisture figure for this specific preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — puttu contains no hydrogenated fats; all fat is from natural coconut and rice flour). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; puttu contains no beta-carotene or retinol sources). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — puttu is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal or fortified sources present). GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for puttu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. GL per serving calculated as: (65 × 27 × 1.5) / 100 = 26.3. The accompaniment (kadala curry vs. banana/palm sugar) dramatically changes the composite meal GI and clinical suitability for T2DM — this is a critical clinical consideration for Kerala Singaporeans.
